rate, but with the greasy wool rate, now 3.46. Use of this formula, they
maintain, would show wool tops still to enjoy a subsidy of 19 per cent.
At the time of imposition of the countervailing duty in 1953, the for-
mula proposed now by the National Wool Manufacturers Association
was considered by the Treasury. It was, however, not adopted for two
main reasons: (1) That it was not believed economically justifiable. In the
absence of an income tax, the exchange rate system in Uruguay (as in vari-
ous other countries) had become an instrument of taxation. Consequently
it could be logically argued that only the difference between the general
weighted average exchange rate and the tops rate constituted a sub-
sidy to the latter. The difference between the average rate and the lower
greasy wool rate could be looked upon as an export tax. (The validity
of this argument is supported by the fact the countervailing duty under
Treasury’s formula has been sufficient to halt shipments of Uruguayan
tops to the U.S.). (2) That it was not administratively practical, since it
would open up the possibility of countervailing duties for a vast array of
products, for example, on exports from Brazil of finished timber which
receives a substantially higher exchange rate than crude timber.
The formula selected by Treasury for imposition of the countervail-
ing duty in 1953 was sanctioned by use again in 1954 when the duty
was lowered from 18 percent to 6 percent, and it is the basis on which
the possible removal of the duty has been discussed by the two govern-
ments in subsequent years.
You are well aware, I know, of the harmful effect which the coun-
tervailing duty has had in our relations with Uruguay in recent years.
That its removal is a matter of great interest to the newly elected gov-
ernment of that country has already been indicated to us by one of the
leading figures in the new group. Serious repercussions would inevita-
bly ensue from our failure to proceed with removal of the countervail-
ing duty on the basis of the Treasury formula.
I therefore believe it is of paramount importance that we do every-
thing we can to encourage Treasury to issue the order removing the
countervailing duty on the scheduled date.

Uruguay  1171
UR–11.
Telegram From the Embassy in Uruguay to the
Department of State
Montevideo, October 6, 1959.
UR–13.
Memorandum of a Conversation
Montevideo, March 2, 1960.
US/MC/32
PARTICIPANTS
United States

Uruguay
The President

Senator Luis BATLLE Berres
Ambassador Robert F. Woodward
Deputy Jorge BATLLE Ibañez
Major John Eisenhower

Sr. Luis BATLLE Ibañez
Colonel Vernon Walters
SUBJECT
Conversation During Courtesy Call of Senator (former President) Luis BATLLE Berres
Senator Luis BATLLE Berres, accompanied by his two sons, Jorge
Batlle Ibañez (who is now a Deputy in the Uruguayan Chamber of
Deputies and the principal administrative manager of the Colorado List
15 newspaper ACCION) and Luis Batlle Ibañez (who is a concert pia-
nist just beginning his career) called on President Eisenhower on March
20 minutes, in the company of Ambassador Robert F. Woodward; Major
John Eisenhower; and Colonel Vernon Walters, the President’s inter-
preter. Senator Batlle inquired about the health of Mrs. Eisenhower and
expressed regret that she had not been able to make the visit to Uruguay
and the other countries of South America. President Eisenhower inquired
about Sra. de Batlle and recalled the pleasant visit that he had received
from former President Batlle and his wife and two sons at the White House
in 1955. The President and Senator Batlle reminisced briefly about Senator
Batlle’s visit to the United States. President Eisenhower remarked on the
very warm reception that he had received from the Uruguayan people
during the afternoon, and then Senator Batlle commented apologetically
about the irresponsible actions of a very small number of university stu-
dents. President Eisenhower replied that it was apparent that the great
majority of the Uruguayans who greeted him were very friendly and that
he was very appreciative of this warm welcome. He said that he did not
consider the student demonstrations of any significance and that he was
not worried about the kind of bombs (tar bombs) which the students were
rumored to be preparing. He alluded, good-­humoredly, to the fact that he
had been exposed to more dangerous bombs than these without ill effects.
Only. Drafted by Ambassador Woodward, and approved in the White House on March
22. Information on the source text indicates that the conversation took place in the U.S.
Embassy residence at 9: a.m.

Uruguay  1173
President Eisenhower commented that those minor criticisms were
nothing as compared with the ones that he has been receiving from Cuba,
and he said that this situation poses a serious problem for the United
States. Senator Batlle said that while Fidel CASTRO had great prestige
throughout Latin America as a revolutionist, he had lost that prestige as a
governor. He repeated this observation three times, wishing to emphasize
it. In the course of emphasizing this point, he said that the countries of
South America could not permit communism to gain control in Cuba. The
President commented that this is really a problem for the Organization
of American States, and the countries of the Western Hemisphere as a
whole. After restarting his views in various ways, which appeared to be
entirely critical of the Cuban Government, Senator Batlle departed.
UR–14.
Memorandum of a Conversation
Montevideo, March 2, 1960.
US/MC/33
SUBJECT
Various
PARTICIPANTS
United States
Uruguayan
The President
President of the National
Mr. Hagerty
  Council of Government,
Secretary Herter
  Sr. Benito Nardone
Major Eisenhower
Councillor Victor Haedo
Ambassador Woodward
Councillor Faustino Harrison
Lt. Col. Walters (Interpreter)
Councillor Martin R. Echegoyen

Councillor Justo M. Alonso

Councillor Pedro Zabalza

Councillor Ledo Arroyo Torres

Councillor Hector A. Grauert

Councillor Cesar Batlle Pacheco

Secretary Manuel Sanchez Morales

Minister of Foreign Relations,

  Homero Martinez Montero

Aide to President Eisenhower,

  General Enrique Magnani
Only. Drafted by Jackson W. Wilson, and approved in the White House on March 24.
Information on the source text indicates that the conversation took place in the Session
Room; Government House, at 4: p.m.

President Nardone opened the discussion with a short explanation
of the nature of the National Council of Government, stating that this
body, with the Minister of Foreign Relations present, constituted the
Government, which welcomed the President of the United States as a
representative of the cradle of democracy and as a defender of all the
democratic institutions of the world.
He called attention to the function of the minority in cross-­checking
the acts of the Government, to Uruguayans’ pride in their democratic
processes, and to the democratic transfer of authority when the previ-
ous Government was defeated at the polls after a long tenure in office.
Uruguayans are devoted to peace, he said, as a matter of national
conscience, believing that only in peace is it possible to make just solu-
tions of the problems of the people. Uruguay has economic and finan-
cial matters to work out but wishes to do this within the pattern of
continental collaboration and welcomes the discussions in the Americas
which have resulted in creation of the Inter-American Bank and other
institutions.
President Nardone, on behalf of the whole council, then invited
President Eisenhower to express his views on international political
problems.
President Eisenhower responded that he would be pleased to
speak from his experience both in the armed forces and as a statesman.
War is absurd and peace is imperative in the present circumstances, he
said, when a great city such as Montevideo could be converted to dust
in seconds. The Soviet Union on one side and the U.S., Great Britain
and France on the other have such destructive power that even a sur-
prise attacker would himself be destroyed. The President repeated that
with nuclear weapons a general war would be absurd. He went on to
say that there is reason for hope for the future but the cost of maintain-
ing deterrent power to prevent aggression is tremendous. It necessarily
involves difficulties for the maintenance of living standards, education
and well-­being of our people. While the smaller nations cannot partic-
ipate in this military activity, all the Western countries must be firmly
united spiritually. The President warned that the Soviets, recognizing
this situation, are going to try to lull the West into a false sense of secu-
rity and will resort to propaganda, subversion and infiltration to seduce
other peoples to deliver up their sovereignty and freedom. He pointed
out that the countries in the greatest danger are those on the Asian and
European periphery, mentioning specifically Laos, Cambodia, India,
Formosa, Viet-Nam, Korea and Iran. We must furnish a great deal of
assistance to prevent the development of movements of disintegration
in the western world and among free peoples.
The President said it is indispensable to give the peoples in Asia
and Africa, living in misery unknown in the Americas, the hope and

Uruguay  1175
the opportunity to improve their living conditions through the type
of assistance which will enable them to attain this objective. At pres-
ent the U.S. is spending 45 billion dollars a year, 60 per cent of its
budget, for military purposes to assure our protection and maintain
the shield behind which our people may work. This year, he said, we
are requesting our Congress to provide $4. billion for programs of
technical assistance, economic aid, and defense support. The capacity
to carry out this program depends upon the maintenance of fiscal sta-
bility and the value of our money, without resorting to inflation. The
U.S. has many internal problems but the most important is to maintain
a healthy economy and prove to the world that we are assuring finan-
cial responsibility. The U.S. will never refuse to aid, nor will abandon,
a friend who is sincerely working, making an effort to secure his live-
lihood and to improve the living conditions of the people. In such
cases, within the framework of its world responsibilities and within
the limitations of its resources, the U.S. will do everything possible to
assist that friend.
President Eisenhower reiterated his conviction that the free world
will have the necessary strength to assure its own security, and con-
cluded by saying he would be pleased to try to answer any specific
questions which the Councillors might wish to ask.
After President Nardone thanked President Eisenhower for his
remarks, the latter said it would be useful to him, on the eve of the new
conferences in Europe2, if he could know if the Councillors had any
objection concerning what the United States was trying to do, if the
U.S. is mistaken in its policies or should modify them. He said that one
of his purposes in visiting Latin America before going to Paris was to
obtain the opinions of these countries. He requested that such views be
expressed to the U.S. Government through official channels.
President Nardone’s statement that the Council shared and wished
to strengthen the policies that are being carried out by the U.S. to defend
democratic principles was seconded by Councillors Arroyo Torres and
Batlle Pacheco of the Opposition. Councillor Haedo commented that,
in this case, he agreed with the Opposition. He went on to say that the
reception given to President Eisenhower would have been duplicated
if he had come as a private citizen. Councillor Batlle Pacheco joined in
this sentiment, saying that the President interprets exactly the North
American nation and its glorious past.
President Nardone then expressed Uruguayan thanks for the col-
laboration of the United States which had restored optimism to their
spirits in the difficult period of the floods, and concluded the meeting
with the presentation to President Eisenhower, on behalf of the NCG,
of a medal as a memento of the occasion.
Diego de Arteaga,

  Uruguay Rascher–civilian

  aide to Secretary Herter
SUBJECT
Official Call on Minister of Foreign Affairs; Cuban Problems - (Separate Memoran-
dum of Conversation have been prepared on “Law of the Sea Hatiters”; “General
World Situation”; and “Exchange of Gifts,”)
Drafted by Henry A. Hoyt on March 3, and approved in the office of the Secretary of State
on March 17, 1960. Information on the source text indicates that the conversation took
place in the Uruguayan Foreign offices at 7: p.m.

Uruguay  1177
There was an exchange of amenities during which the Foreign
Minister expressed the hope that the personal relationship which had
been developed with the Secretary beginning with the Santiago FM
Conference would be considered as evidence of the friendly and coop-
erative relationship which the Uruguayan Government wished to have
with the United States.
The Secretary then brought up the question of Cuba which he had
touched upon briefly with the Foreign Minister in the motorcade earlier
in the day. The Secretary pointed out that the Cuban situation poses
a very serious problem for the United States, but that it is not just a
problem for the United States but one for all of the American Republics.
The Secretary said he would appreciate having the Foreign Minister’s
views of the situation. The Secretary said that since the withdrawal of
our Ambassador from Cuba there had been a lessening of attacks on the
United States; on the Secretary; on Assistant Secretary Rubottom and
on Ambassador Bonsal. Nevertheless, the Cuban Government had con-
tinued to place in important positions within the Government persons
who were known to be of the extreme left, if not Communists. This is
of concern to the United States because it may indicate that the Cuban
Government is likely to follow on extremist course and take extremist
actions which will further complicate our relations, despite a reduction
in criticism of the United States. The Secretary said that it would be
hard to predict just where such a course might end.
The Secretary made it very plain that we are not concerned with
Cuba’s Agrarion Reform Law. He said that this is a domestic matter for
the Cubans to determine. All that we ask is that there be just, adequate
and prompt payment to any Americans whose property is taken. In
this convection, the Secretary pointed out that the Cubans have just
announced that all further foreign capital investment will be prohib-
ited. This might be a further indication of a trend which is developing
within the Cuban Government. Such a law would, of course, be of con-
cern not only to the United States but to all other foreign countries.
The Secretary also explained that the Cubans are desirous of nego-
tiating with us on certain matters, and had recently proposed such
negotiations but with conditions which were one-­sided and completely
unacceptable to the United States. We have replied to the Cubans that
we would not be able to negotiate on this basis. The Secretary pointed
out that the unrealistic and unilateral approach of the Cubans on the
question of negotiations might be a further indication of the trend of
events in Cuba, and certainly will have a hearing on our relations with
that country.
The Foreign Minister at this point, after indicating surprise at the
news of this new Cuban action of foreign investments, said he under-
stood completely that the Cuban situation presented a very difficult

problem for the United States, particularly because of the geographic
proximity of Cuba to the United States. He said he believed, however,
that it was not just a problem of Cuba but of the whole Caribbean area.
He referred particularly to the Dominican Republic (called it Santo
Domingo) as another part of the Caribbean problem. The Minister
said he believed that it would be necessary for the United States to
try and determine the causes of the trouble in the area. He suspected
that these causes were economic and that a settlement of the economic
situation might have important repercussion in the political field in
the Caribbean.
The Secretary then commented on the problems in our relations
with Cuba which are of immediate concern and are important because
of the effect they may have on our relations with that country. First, it
will soon be necessary to determine whether or not to send Ambassador
Bonsal back to Cuba. If the decision is made to send him back, and he
is then subjected once again to insults or is hindered in carrying out his
official mission, he would be withdrawn very very quickly. Obviously
this problem and its solution could have an important hearing on nor-
mal relations with Cuba. Secondly, there is the question of the Sugar
Act which expires this year. The Secretary emphasized that this is better
for Congress to decide and that the Executive Branch, therefore, cannot
predict the outcome. The Secretary outlined for the Foreign Minister
the advantages which Cuba obtains through its special position in our
market including the sale of three million tons of sugar at the United
States domestic price which is about 2 cents above world price. This
means about 150 million dollars to Cuba annually.
The Foreign Minister again expressed his understanding of the
very difficult problem. He said that Uruguay also had been studying
the problem very carefully as evidenced by the fact that Uruguay had
not as yet replied to the approach made by the Cuban Ambassadors
who were here last month and who had invited Cuba to attend the con-
ference on under-­developed countries. Martinez Montero did not state
categorically that Uruguay’s reply would be negative although he did
give that implication.
The Foreign Minister pointed out that Cuba is very active in
espousing its own cause, and that we can expect a continuance of
Cuban activity in this respect. He mentioned that Cuba had, after con-
siderable length of time, suddenly named diplomatic representatives to
vacant posts, including Uruguay. He also said that the 150th anniver-
sary of Argentina’s independence might furnish another propaganda
forum for the Cubans because Argentina is inviting the countries of
the hemisphere to attend celebrations of this anniversary. The Minister
concluded by expressing the hope that the United States, through its
“very able diplomacy” would be able to solve the Caribbean problem.

Uruguay  1179
SUMMARY:
Although the conversation offered a very good opportunity for the
Secretary to explain fully our positions vis-à-­vis Cuba, the Uruguayan
Foreign Minister did not respond with any assurances that Uruguay
understood it was a hemisphere problem nor did he give any indication
of any official Uruguayan position on the Cuban case. He did make the
point that, in the minds of the Uruguayans, the Cuban and Dominican
problems are linked, and should be considered together. This, of course,
was a reflection of the very strong anti-Dominican attitude in Uruguay.
The Minister soon wondered off the Cuban question and started talking
in generalities about the world of situation. Whether this was deliber-
ate or merely represented his lack of coordinated thinking on the prob-
lem is conjectural.
UR–16.
Despatch from the Ambassador in Uruguay (Woodward)
to the Department of State
Montevideo, August 26, 1960.
No. 155.
SUBJECT
Current Political and Economic Situation in Uruguay
Summary
Relations between the Uruguayan Government and the United
States Government are at present very cooperative. This is important
to the United States because Uruguay still possesses special interna-
tional prestige. And Uruguayan governmental cooperation is well sup-
ported by Uruguayan public opinion. There is increasing awareness
in Uruguay of the danger of international communism; Uruguayans
consider Uruguay impregnable to communism; but communist and
Cuban propaganda is persistent, and communist influence in Uruguay
is strong among a minority of the labor movement and a minority of
the teachers.
The present Uruguayan Administration is definitely more conser-
vative than the preceding one. It is slowly stabilizing the economic and

monetary situation of the country, slowly restoring agricultural pro-
ductivity and slowly rectifying the imbalance in international trade.
Whether it will succeed in doing this sufficiently to win again in the
November 1962 elections is a moot point and may depend in part upon
economic development projects in which foreign credit assistance may
be an important factor. Also, economic progress in the next few years
depends in large part upon greater agricultural productivity (and luck
with world prices) and the agricultural potential in Uruguay appears
rather limited.
Uruguay has had an interesting and unusual economic and social
development in the past but has tended to stagnate somewhat because
of inefficient use of manpower, lack of leadership, and lack of pro-
ductivity to pay the bill for social services. Although “welfare-­state”
services and public ownership of utilities and industries have been
conspicuous, the economy is surprisingly capitalistic and ownership
fairly heavily concentrated. While economic stability will probably be
achieved and economic activity in the country substantially improved,
there is considerable question about the possibility of Uruguay’s being
able to equal the future progress of the larger South American countries
with more varied resources.
The domestic political prospects are largely conjectural, with no
clear indication of outstanding prospective leaders and as yet no basis
for predicting which parties may gain more public favor than others.
In this state of flux, however, the probabilities of continued orientation
of the Government toward the United States and the Western world
are good.
Political Relations with the United States
Relations between the Uruguayan Government and the United
States are very good to excellent. The present (Blanco) Administration
which came into power on March 1, 1959 is thoroughly convinced that
international cooperation with the United States and the Western world
is advantageous to Uruguay, and the only natural and consistent line
for Uruguay to take in view of its own long political and economic tra-
ditions. This is a notable improvement over the situation that existed
during the preceding (Colorado) Administration. The main segments
of both large Uruguayan political parties, as well as the Catholic (Union
Civica) party all have extremist fringes that are inclined to be national-
istic and to temporize with communism—in the sense that they tend,
in the Cuban style, to sympathize with efforts to improve the living
standards of the masses, even at the cost of dictatorship for the prole-
tariat, the subordination of civil liberties, and the ignoring of private
(particularly foreign) property rights. But the two main segments of the
Blanco party have far smaller such fringes than the List 15 Colorados,
the larger of the two main Colorado segments.

Uruguay  1181
The smaller of the two main segments of the Colorado party (List
14) is more moderate in its economic and social views and has been con-
sistently friendly toward the United States for years, as exemplified by
the utterances of its newspaper EL DIA. Even the List 15 segment of the
Colorado party has been at least as cooperative in its attitude toward
the United States since it left office on March 1, 1959 as it was during
its own Administration. Evidently, the main body of List 15—­except for
the extremist fringe—­appears as convinced as are the Blanco groups
that cooperation with the United States and the Western World is the
only natural and consistent line for Uruguay. And even the extremist
fringe of List 15 is inclined to admire many aspects of the United States
and its policies while likewise taking an interest in other methods of
bettering the lot of the masses.
Therefore, the majority of Uruguayan citizens clearly support the
attitude of the Uruguayan Administration toward the United States.
(Perhaps the most important effect of the visit to Uruguay of President
Eisenhower was to precipitate a display of attitudes which showed
all Uruguayans vividly that the great majority of themselves approve
of the United States and its policies. This, and the converse discovery
that militant critics were a small minority, has tended to give many
Uruguayans more courage and more conviction and more inclination
to align themselves openly with the democratic ideology as symbolized
by the United States.)
Successful United States efforts to clear away points of irritation
in Uruguayan-U.S. relations, and to develop cooperative programs
between the two countries have undoubtedly been a substantial factor
in the improvement of relations, although the advent of the Blanco
Administration on March 1, 1959 was undoubtedly the most import-
ant factor.
Importance to the United States of Good Relations with Uruguay
Although Uruguay does not stand out among other nations as
conspicuously as three or four decades ago, it is still especially use-
ful to have Uruguayan support for United States policies and actions.
Uruguay’s international reputation as a country of democratic, edu-
cated, and liberty-­loving people has given Uruguayan attitudes and
views more weight at international meetings or in international public
discussions than the attitudes and views of any other small country of
the Western Hemisphere. Uruguay continues to be the most outstand-
ing small country in the Western Hemisphere in many respects: It has
the most firmly-­entrenched democratic traditions and institutions,
including a permanent Electoral Court to supervise all national elec-
tions. It has the most democratic form of Government in the American
Republics in that there is the least opportunity for any one man to
attain conspicuous authority. It has a practically completely graft-­free

Government. It has the most complete religious freedom and the most
absolute separation of church and State. It has had professional armed
services, divorced from participation in politics, for longer than any
other Latin American country. It probably has a smaller percentage
of illiterates and a larger percentage of newspaper readers than any
other Latin American country—­with a deeper devotion to all forms of
civil liberties.
Uruguayan Attitudes toward Communism and the Soviet Bloc
Those Uruguayans who have been interested in the professed
advances of communist States in guaranteeing at least minimum
“social security” to their populations are now becoming aware of the
ruthless dictatorial and police methods that have been required to do
this and of the total immorality of communism whenever this serves
communist purposes in relations with countries outside of the Bloc.
These Uruguayans are confident that their country is basically impreg-
nable to the dictatorial or police-­state ideas of communism. But, since
such ideas have begun to take hold in Cuba, their confidence is begin-
ning to be shaken. Many Uruguayans, moreover, have seemed to have
an attitude bordering on irresponsibility about the threat of communist
infiltration and subversion in democratic and uncommitted countries
in other parts of the world. For example, some Uruguayans have not
seemed to realize that visits of curiosity to the Soviet Bloc have the effect
of subtly enhancing Soviet prestige and lulling gullible peoples in other
countries into less concern about the dangers of communist propa-
ganda, infiltration and agitation. It is to be hoped—­particularly in view
of the developments in Cuba—­that such Uruguayans are beginning to
understand that any act of theirs which shows intellectual benevolence
toward communist countries has the effect of raising communist pres-
tige and endangering other more vulnerable democratic peoples not
only on the periphery of the Bloc but even closer to Uruguay.
After completely free political activity since 1920, the Uruguayan
Communist Party won 27,000 votes out of one million votes cast in the
November 1958 national elections. The most the Communists have ever
won was 34,000 in 1946. Uruguayans have not seemed to realize that
other poorer and less-­educated peoples in other countries would be far
more susceptible to communist blandishments if this same degree of
freedom of activity were to be permitted.
The steady cumulative effect of news stories and other sources of
information has undoubtedly tended to make Uruguayans more realis-
tic, but Communist is persistent and communist influence continues to
be strong in Uruguay in a minority of the labor movement and among
a minority of the teachers.

Uruguay  1183
Uruguayan Economic Improvement
The present Uruguayan Administration has moved slowly to
improve the country’s economic situation. It has succeeded so far in
stabilizing the international value of the Uruguayan peso, in taking
a major step to eliminate the system of multiple exchange rates with
many beneficial results, in coming closer to balancing the budget, in
significantly restricting credit, in stopping the drain on the gold bal-
ances of the central bank, and in slowing down the increase in the cost
of living. A major benefit from the elimination of the system of multi-
ple exchange rates was to place international trade at the beginning of
1960 on a completely multilateral basis and eliminate bilateralism and
discrimination. Another benefit was to increase the effective returns
from important export products, creating new incentives for additional
production. Another benefit to the economy was the elimination of
costly subsidies of certain imports, both for consumption and for aid
to industry.
The Blanco Administration has shown an admirable steadfastness
of purpose in proceeding with steps toward economic stabilization,
over the obstacle of much clamor from the Opposition and particularly
the Communists and their Socialist allies. Moreover, slow governmen-
tal procedures had been made slower by many differences of opinion
within the Blanco ranks and by the exceedingly slim Blanco majority
in the Legislature—50 out of 99 members in the Chamber of Deputies.
Much of the credit for the progress which has been made is due to the
Minister of Finance, Contador Juan AZZINI, who has a clear concep-
tion of his course, and to the comparative political imperturbability
of the two main characters in the nine-­man Presidential Council (the
National Council of Government), Chairman Benito NARDONE and
next year’s Chairman, Victor HAEDO.
The steady inflation that had come about from years of overspend-
ing and subsidizing favored urban and industrial elements in the pop-
ulation has had the natural effect of raising living costs in comparison
with individual income. After having spent and subsidized for years to
perpetuate itself in office, the List 15 Colorado Administration became
discredited because the voters began to discover that they had to pay
the bill through reduced individual real income. And then, ironically,
the one period in which this trend was felt the most keenly was the part
of 1959 after the Blanco Administration took over on March 1.
In recent months, wage increases in many industries have been
greater than cost-­of-­living increases, tending partly to restore real
income. The Government’s four-­year budget is about to be passed by the
Legislature with substantial wage increases for Government employ-
ees, which anticipate to some extent the fact that increases cannot be

made again for four years. A law recently passed modestly increases a
large proportion of existing pensions to make income catch up at least
in part with increased prices. When these and various other pending
adjustments in salaries and wages are made, it is the Government’s
intention to adopt a wage policy, that, henceforth, wage increases shall
not be larger than increases in the cost-­of-­living index.
It will remain to be seen whether the Blanco Administration can
promote sufficient improvement in the economy of Uruguay before
the next national elections in November 1962 to be able to win. This
will depend partly upon the success of the Administration in develop-
ing sound economic development projects and obtaining the financing
for them.
The success of the Blanco Administration will also depend in
part upon World demand and prices for wool and beef and upon the
increased quantities of these and other products that can be produced
in Uruguay. Uruguayan agriculture—­particularly the wool indus-
try—was heavily taxed through low exchange rates and therefore low
receipts in the multiple exchange-­rate system. Agriculture was forced
to operate upon such a low-­cost basis that large farms became larger
with fewer workers, which brought about a decline in total agricultural
production and even in the number of individual farm operators. The
small farmers were badly squeezed and became the most neglected
identifiable segment of the population.
The present Uruguayan Administration represents, in part, a
reaction to this situation. The Herrerista Blancos, who now control
the Executive branch of the Government, have a preponderance of
their supporters in the rural areas and the small towns. And Sr. Benito
Nardone, whose Liga Federal or “Ruralists” joined forces with the
Herreristas in the last elections, is the spokesman and leader of the
small farmers who vigorously protested their lot.
Undoubtedly, considerable improvement can be made in
Uruguayan agricultural productivity without increasing costs more
than increasing productivity, and this possibility will be enhanced by
progress in the science. However, the additional effort and manage-
ment required on the part of ranchers who have been accustomed to
extensive rather than intensive techniques will retard such individuals
who do not see much prospect of individual profit—­even though the
nation as a whole may badly need increased productivity and export
trade. Moreover, Uruguay’s natural advantages for beef production are
not as great as those of Argentina, and the small size of the country and
modest quality of the soil place a natural limitation on the expansion of
Uruguay’s agricultural exports.

Uruguay  1185
Uruguay’s Unusual Economic and Social Situation; Future Prospects
Despite a degree of inflation which has increased prices more than
it has wages, Uruguay probably still possesses a larger percentage of
population who are fed, clothed and sheltered reasonably well than any
other Latin American country. An ECLA study showed that, in 1958,
about 78% of the Uruguayan population had a productivity of over 400
US$ per capita, which was the highest recorded in Latin America.
Uruguay has long had the international reputation of having the
largest proportion of pensioners, the largest variety of other social
security services, the largest proportion of Government employees, the
most public ownership of utilities and industries, and the most “wel-
fare state” institutions in general of any Latin American country. On a
comparative basis, this is probably true. For example, there are about
200,000 pensioners and about 100,000 others who are eligible but who
have not been able to obtain pensions as yet because of delays that are
fundamentally due to depleted pension funds that have made loans to
the Government. There is a system of small “family allowances” which
are funded like pensions and supplement wages, and there are unem-
ployment funds. For medical and hospital services, the population is
about equally divided between those whose income is low enough to
make them eligible for public health services and those who are mem-
bers of one of the many “mutual” health insurance societies; it is esti-
mated that only about 5% of Uruguayans now depend entirely upon the
private direct purchase of medical and hospital services. All of the utili-
ties in the country except the (British-­owned) Montevideo gas company
are now owned by the Government. And the Government monopolizes
petroleum refining, most liquor distilling, a large part of gasoline dis-
tribution, a large part of cement manufacturing, fertilizer and chemical
manufacturing, fishing and fish distribution, and insurance.
There is very little foreign ownership of industry in Uruguay and
the funded foreign debt is well under $100 million USCy.
Despite the reputation of Uruguay, however, the backbone of its
economy is private capitalism, ownership is concentrated in a sur-
prisingly small proportion of the population, and the economic and
social attitudes of these capitalists are, on the whole, not conspicuously
enlightened. About 35% of all the arable land in the country is con-
centrated in the hands of about 600 families, and the concentration of
the ownership of industry is probably about the same. Farm workers
average less than $20 USCy, a month plus room and board, and ordi-
nary industrial workers average from $60 to $80 USCy, a month, plus
about 20% more from the employer for pension funds and other social
security payments.
Also, some of the wealth that has built Montevideo into an impres-
sive city for such a small country has come from persons who have

found Uruguay a convenient financial “safe-­haven’’ and Uruguayan
tax laws have encouraged the establishment of holding companies
which have their investments elsewhere. There has also been a con-
siderable tourist business, mostly from Argentina, and a considerable
number of political refugees—Argentines and others—­have invested in
residential and business property in Uruguay.
Along with this situation, there has been an interesting but small-­
scale development of intellectual and artistic life in Uruguay—­which
has strangely seemed to parallel the “safe-­haven” spirit in a degree
of isolationism and an anachronistic clinging by small groups, to out-
moded political ideas such as anarchism and early Marxian socialism.
Uruguayan economic and social (and intellectual) development
seem largely to have stood still in the past 15 or 20 years, as neigh-
boring countries have developed. This has been partly due to the
inefficiency of government operations which have wasted manpower
egregiously, with the natural result that the public has had to pay the
bill one way or another. Stating this in another way, so much manpower
has been wasted that productivity has not been sufficient to pay the bill
for goods and services, with the result that they have been paid for by
credit expansion and a slow drain on the gold reserves and the result-
ing inflation which made the public pay.
The present Uruguayan Administration is definitely determined to
make the Government and the Government business operations more
efficient, and considerable progress will undoubtedly be made in this
direction. The amount of progress that will be made, however, is a moot
point, since the lack of stimuli to and checks on the Government utility
and industrial operations is much less than on private corporations.
There is some sentiment among leaders of the present Administration
actually favoring the sale to private owners of certain Government
business operations, but it is very unlikely that this will be done, even
with the least efficient such as the fishing industry. The most that can be
expected is that some increase in private enterprise may be encouraged.
The prospects for significant economic progress in Uruguay, once
Uruguayan agriculture is brought up to optimum productivity, do not
appear to be outstanding. There may, however, be many ways in which
Uruguay can profit from closer economic integration with Argentina,
and this may offer the promising possibility for real future economic
progress. Uruguay has suffered from a lack of products which the
Argentines are interested in buying. And the most obvious way in which
Uruguayans could profit from an increasingly prosperous Argentina
would be to devise every feasible means of attracting larger numbers
of Argentine tourists; Argentine tourists already spend a substantial
amount every year in Uruguay, and it might be possible for Uruguay
to attract large enough numbers in the future to create enough foreign

Uruguay  1187
exchange to purchase Uruguayan petroleum needs from Argentina.
This exchange of tourist services for petroleum would be greatly facil-
itated by the existence of a bridge or viaduct between Buenos Aires
and Colonia in Uruguay (or a bridge somewhat farther upstream), and
the financing of this construction project—­a viaduct with a petroleum
pipeline attached to it—­might therefore be one of the most interesting
objects of international financial cooperation to consider with a view
to the encouraging and hastening of an inevitable long-­term trend in
Uruguay an economic development.
This Embassy has hoped that it might be possible for Uruguay to
develop highly skilled industries, because there are many very capa-
ble Uruguayan craftsmen and mechanics. However, the Embassy is
inclined now to estimate that the prospects of the development of truly
outstanding industries are rather poor, and the principal hope in this
respect is that there may be greater development of the local indus-
tries which process Uruguayan raw materials, such as the woolen tex-
tile industry.
Attempting to project Uruguayan economic development in line
with a realistic estimate of the situation which will probably exist ten
years from now, it can be predicted that Uruguayans will be, on the
average, only moderately better off than they are at present, and that
any difference in their average situation will correlate quite closely with
a possibly improved wool market (which, in turn, may be dependent
upon new marketing opportunities that may develop in the United
States from the Australian-United States conversations which have
been recently initiated.)
Here follows a discussion of domestic political trends.
Regardless of domestic political trends in Uruguay, the prospects
of continuing cooperative attitudes in relations with the United States
and the Western World are good. These prospects could be reduced (1)
by our not giving reasonable economic cooperation to Uruguay (2) by
our falling behind, as a nation, in the worldwide economic, sociological,
ideological and defense contest, or (3) by our departing from the inter-
American machinery to take unilateral action in some situation such as
in the Cuban case. However, assuming that we will devise increasingly
effective means of economic cooperation, that we will continue to make
every effort to make inter-American machinery more effective, and that
our country will steadily gain in the worldwide contest, I believe we
can count on Uruguayan political and ideological support.
Robert F. Woodward

UR–17.
Memorandum of the Conversation
Washington, September 20, 1960.
SUBJECT
Meeting Between Sr. Haedo, Member of the National Council of Uruguay and
Forthcoming President of that country2 and President Eisenhower, September
PARTICIPANTS
United States

Uruguay
President Dwight D. Eisenhower

National Councilor Haedo
Ambassador Robert F. Woodward
Ambassador Carlos A. Clulow
Mr. C. A. Boonstra, ARA
Mr. F. vanReigersburg, LS
The President at 11 a.m. welcomed Councilor Haedo and men-
tioned the painting, on a chair in the office, by Manuel Rosé entitled,
“Sunset in the Sierra de Minas” which the Councilor had sent as a gift
to the President.
After being seated, Councilor Haedo opened the conversation by
remarking jocularly about Uruguay’s reputation as a country in which
all sorts of radical movements flourished such as Castroism, Peronism,
and Communism. In fact, he said, this is not the case. Uruguay is a
small and stable country, but in common with other countries it has
a need for improved internal security. To enhance this, he suggested
that it may be a good idea to hold a meeting of police experts from
all of the Latin American countries in Montevideo, “something like an
Interpol meeting”. This could provide an opportunity for a discussion
of police measures needed to improve internal security. To the extent
possible such a meeting should be without publicity. At the same time
it is becoming necessary to consider in Uruguay the provision of small
arms for the police. In this connection he referred to the program of mil-
itary assistance and suggested that present regulations both for military
assistance and for the Uruguayan military forces should be interpreted
more broadly so that the necessary small arms could be provided to
the police. Even above these conditions, however, the Councilor said
that he wished to emphasize the basic importance of financial security.
by Clarence A. Boenstra, Director of the Office of East Coast Affairs, and Fernando A. Van
[illegible in the original] of the Division of language Services. Information on the [illegi-
ble in the original] was approved in the White House on September 23.

Uruguay  1189
Means must be found to provide the type of financial and economic
arrangements on which there could be established an internally secure
situation.
The President asked about the existing Uruguayan situation in
other respects. The Councilor replied that everything is fine and that
“with Bogotá, the arrangements with the International Monetary
Fund—that have been very helpful—­and financial assistance from the
United States” there will be no problems. He said that his reports from
the Uruguayan delegation to the Bogotá Conference were that the con-
ference was very successful. The President remarked that the success of
the Bogotá Conference was most pleasing to him.
The President the said that he would like to mention a situation
which is giving him a great deal of worry. He recognizes the need for
economic growth in Latin America and he is convinced that private
investment for this purpose is more useful than public investment.
During his trip earlier this year to South America, leaders in the var-
ious countries agreed that private investment is urgently needed. The
excesses of Castro in Cuba, however, are now establishing a situation
which deters investors from making investments in other countries of
Latin America. This has been brought to the President’s personal atten-
tion of late in conversations with individuals who would normally be
making such investments.
The President said that there may be a lack of realization in Latin
America of the extent to which Cuba now is an obstacle to private
investment. He said that he would like to see the American govern-
ments undertake the kind of programs needed to support a greater
flow of capital in spite of Castro. The ridiculous demand of Castro for
$30 billion dollars is illustrative of the wrong way to go about this. He
then invited the Councilor to make known his views on the problem.
The Councilor responded with lengthy remarks respecting Castro
and what he termed the United States problem with Castro. He said
that obviously much violence and misunderstanding has taken place
and that in his opinion it has become necessary to seek means for clar-
ifying the situation. He does not think that this can be done by the
United States or within the framework of the United Nations. Instead
it is a problem which must be taken up by countries in Latin America
which are less directly involved, particularly Uruguay which is a coun-
try above suspicion of domination by any foreign power. Direct or uni-
lateral action by the United States would be misunderstood and would
cause hostility among the American Republics.
The President responded by stating that violence would appear
to be unilateral on the part of the Castro Government, and that the
United States has been very patient. It is difficult to understand why
Castro behaves the way he does. Cuba’s prosperity depends on its

friendship with the United States and there is no quarrel at all between
the Cuban and the United States peoples. Castro has behaved as a wild
man, contrary to the interests of Cuba and of the hemisphere; and now
has become linked to the Soviet bloc. The irresponsible seizure of prop-
erty is having a direct impact upon investments in the other American
Republics and the latter should be gravely concerned. The President
recognized the appeal of the initial Castro revolutionary program
then as agrarian reform, particularly the students and related groups
throughout the hemisphere, but [illegible in the original] that [illegi-
ble in the original] groups should be [illegible in the original] equally
at least with the need for private investment as a means for economic
growth. Also the President said that the direct Cuban-United States
problem can be considered a small one, and although small problems
can be much more of a nuisance than big ones, his principal concern is
with the broader implications which originate from it.
The Councilor interrupted frequently during the course of the
President’s [illegible in the original] in order to present his views. In
essence, the Councilor [illegible in the original] must be found to push
Castro against the wall and [illegible in the original] him to state his
position, clearly once and for all, on his attitude toward the United
States and toward the other American Republics [illegible in the origi-
nal] what it is that he wants. This can be achieved, he said by [illegible
in the original] a committee of four or five nations, including Uruguay,
to discuss these matters directly with Castro. The Committee should
express the concern of all of the American Republics regarding U.S.-
Cuban relations and ask Castro where he stands, if he [illegible in the
original] a solution, what can be done, and [illegible in the original] to
solve the present crisis. A final objective would be to make him choose
between the American Republics and the Soviet bloc. In response to
the President’s remarks to the effect that Castro may already [illegible
in the original] quite clear where he stands, the Councilor insisted that
Castro’s [illegible in the original] may be clear to the United States and
perhaps to some other [illegible in the original] groups but that it is not
clear to the Latin American peoples. The Councilor continued to stress
the need for a committee of nations which could force Castro into a
final declaration, to be understood by everyone.
Toward the close of the conversation, the President referred to his
faith and confidence in the United Nations but indicated that he had
as much or more regard for the Organization of American States as a
regional entity of fundamental importance to the United States and to
all of the American Republics. The President said that he hoped, before
leaving office, that he would be able to take actions demonstrating
further the importance which he attaches to the OAS. He has made
plans to visit the United Nations and, during his visit there, he hopes

Uruguay  1191
to invite the Chiefs of Delegations of the other American Republics to
a luncheon, taking advantage of this opportunity to express his high
regard for the OAS countries. The Councilor applauded enthusiasti-
cally these remarks.
At this point, while photographers came in to take pictures, the
conversation turned to the 9-man National Council of Government in
Uruguay and Mr. Haedo jokingly suggested a similar system for the
United States so that all of the candidates could become President for a
year, as he himself would be doing next March 1.
Upon leaving at 11: a.m., the Councilor mentioned again his
hopes that the President would enjoy the painting which he presented
and he added that he would be most gratified if the President might
find it possible to send him an autographed photograph and one of his
own paintings. He reiterated his admiration for the President and his
satisfaction at having been received by him. The President thanked him
for making his friendly visit.
UR–18.
Editorial Note
On October 10, 1960, the International Monetary Fund announced
that an initial par value for the Uruguayan peso had been estab-
lished at 7. pesos per United States dollar, by agreement between
the Fund and the Government of Uruguay. The fund also announced
that it had entered into a stand-­by agreement enabling the Uruguayan
Government to draw the equivalent of United States $30,000,000 over a
12-­month period. Documentation concerning IMF activities in Uruguay

Venezuela: U.S. Relations with Venezuela
VE–1.
Memorandum from the Assistant Secretary of State
for Inter– American Affairs (Rubottom) to the Acting
Secretary of State
Washington, January 28, 1958
SUBJECT
Recognition of the new Venezuelan Government.
Discussion:
Venezuela has undergone a revolutionary change of government.
The former President, General Marcos Pérez Jiménez, was forced by
the Armed Forces to resign on January 23. His downfall was preceded
by an unsuccessful uprising on January 1 and 2, in which the Air Force
and units of the Army sought to overthrow his government. After sev-
eral days of civilian riots and student demonstrations, followed by a
widely supported general strike on January 21 and 22, he was ousted by
Carl E. Bartch of the Office of South American Affairs; concurred in by Robert Murphy,
Deputy Under Secretary of State for Political Affairs; E. Allan Lightner, Deputy Assistant
Secretary of State for International Information and Cultural Affairs; John S. Hoghland,
Deputy Assistant Secretary of State for Congressional Relations; Marjorie M. Whiteman,
Assistant Legal Adviser for Inter-American Affairs; and Clement E. Conger, Assistant Chief
of Protocol.
Secretary Rubottom stated the following with respect to the overthrow of Pérez Jiménez:
“14. Mr. Rubottom gave a full report on recent developments in Venezuela leading
to the overthrow of Perez Jimenez and the establishment of a military junta. He pointed
out that no Americans were involved in these events and that the wells and refineries of
the Standard Oil Company are in full production. He noted that a representative of the
junta called at the Embassy this morning and assued our Charge that the new regime is
friendly to the U.S. and seeks quick recognition.
“Mr. Murphy asked Mr. Rubottom to stay in close contact with the Navy Department
to furnish guidance with respect to the U.S. carrier and destroyers which are approxi-
mately 150 miles from the Venezuelan coast. The Acting Secretary asked Mr. Rubottom
to furnish General Goodpaster at the White House with a summary of the situation in
Venezuela for the information of the President.”
Secretary’s Staff Meetings, Lot 63 D 75)

Venezuela  1193
the Armed Forces. General Pérez Jiménez was a member of the original
three-­man Junta of Government that assumed power in 1948, following a
military coup d’etat that overthrew the legally constituted government of
President Gallegos, who had gained a majority of the popular vote in the
free elections conducted in Venezuela the preceding year. Installed by the
Armed Forces as Provisional President in 1952, following the dissolution
of the Junta, General Pérez Jiménez was named Constitutional President
the following year by the Constituent Assembly. He was “reelected” for
another five-­year term of office as a result of a plebiscite held on December
15, 1957, in which he was the only presidential candidate. Resentment
among the younger military officers and the civilian population over the
plebiscite is believed to have contributed to a great extent to his downfall.
In a note dated January 25, 1958, the new Venezuelan Government
informed our Embassy at Caracas that, in accordance with the univer-
sal desires of the nation, in defense of the supreme interests of the
Republic, and in order to move toward a legally constituted democratic
form of government, the National Armed Forces had assumed power
and had formed a five-man Junta of Government headed by Admiral
Wolfgang Larrazábal, as President of the Junta, and comprised of two
other military officers and two civilians. The note stated that the new
government would comply strictly with its international obligations
and expressed its desire to resume cordial relations with the U.S.
The Junta is in control of the machinery of government. It appears
to enjoy wide popular as well as united military support and there is
no substantial resistance to it. It has pledged by public announcement
to conduct free elections as soon as possible in order to constitute a
demo­cratic form of government. A member of the Junta has assured
our Embassy of the new government’s receptivity to foreign invest-
ment and of its willingness and ability to protect it. He has also given
assurances that the new government will be anti-­communist.
In the belief that early recognition on our part would contribute mate-
rially to the stability of the new regime, we propose that the U.S. recognize,
at 6. p.m. today, the Junta as the Provisional Government of Venezuela.
Recommendation:
That you sign the attached memorandum to the President4 recom-
mending recognition of the Junta as the Provisional Government of the
Republic of Venezuela.
VE–2.
Memorandum from the Assistant Secretary of State
for Inter-American Affairs (Rubottom) to the Acting
Secretary of State.
Washington, January 28, 1958.
SUBJECT
Visa Applications for ‘General Marcos Pérez Jiménez, Former President of
­Venezuela, and his Family.
Background:
The Embassy in Ciudad Trujillo, in telegram No. 326 of January 27,
(Tab B) reports that it is reliably informed that General Marcos Pérez
Jiménez, former President of Venezuela, plans to apply for United
States visas for himself, his wife and mother-­in-­law, four children, and
a servant.
It has been our practice in the absence of extraordinary circum-
stances to grant visas to those exiled political leaders from Latin
America who meet the general requirements of our visa laws and reg-
ulations. Many of the important Venezuelan political figures now re-­
emerging in the Venezuelan political scene enjoyed the hospitality of
the United States during the ten year period of General Pérez Jiménez’
Government. Ex-President Romulo Betancourt was actually admitted
in 1954 over the vehement protests of the Pérez Jiménez Government
on the basis of our established practice.
Although the authoritarian character and the antagonism of the
U.S. press toward the Government of General Pérez Jiménez make it
likely that his admission into the United States would produce con-
siderable press criticism, it is not believed that the circumstances
surrounding this case are such as to justify denial of a visa. He is the
bearer of the Legion of Merit, Degree of Chief Commander, which was
bestowed upon him on November 12, 1954 by President Eisenhower.
This decoration was bestowed upon him in recognition of his collabora-
tion with the United States both internationally and in the development
of Venezuela’s important and strategic petroleum resources through
by Andrew B. Wardlaw, Officer in Charge of North Coast Affairs in the Office of South
American Affairs, and initialled by Deputy Under Secretary Murphy.

Venezuela  1195
private United States and British enterprise. His policies of complete
support for private enterprise were frequently cited as an example in
countries addicted to Socialistic practices.
To lessen the possibility that General Pérez Jiménez may wish
to engage in subversive activity against the Venezuelan Government
while in the United States, the consular officer handling his visa appli-
cation will be instructed to require of General Pérez Jiménez verbal
assurances that he will not undertake such activities during his stay in
our country. A similar course of action was followed with ex-President
Betancourt of Venezuela and is being followed in connection with the
issuance of visas to other prominent Venezuela exiles who seek to enter
the United States at this time.
Recommendation:
That you approve the attached telegram authorizing the issuance
of visas General Pérez Jiménez and his family. (Tab A)

Washington, February 13, 1958.
Dear Mr. Murphy:
This will confirm my telephone conversation with you in which
I expressed my concern regarding the issuance of a visa by the
Department of State to the ex-Dictator of Venezuela, Marcos Perez
Jimenez. As I told you, I learned of the issuance of this visa for the first
time in a newspaper account.
In view of the commitment made by the Department of State, the
Immigration and Naturalization Service, of course, will admit Perez
Jimenez if he applies for admission to the United States. However,
Jimenez—Estrada.” Confidential.

Venezuela  1197
the problems we have encountered in a similar case where a visa was
granted to a foreign political leader prompt me to express the reserva-
tions I mentioned to you in our telephone conversation. In the event
Perez Jimenez, as in the Prio case, should seek to use this country for
future power plotting and the Department of State should protest to this
Department, as was done in the Prio case, I want to make it clear that
it may be difficult to effect his deportation or even to prevent him from
engaging in activities detrimental to our foreign relations while here.
Furthermore, since we talked about this matter, I noticed a United
Press dispatch in this morning’s newspapers reporting that the Assistant
Attorney General of Venezuela has filed a formal petition for the extra-
dition to Venezuela of Pedro Estrada, former chief of the Security Police
under the Perez Jimenez regime. I am advised that Estrada is currently
in the United States on a diplomatic visitor’s visa.
Sincerely yours,
William P. Rogers

VE–5.
Memorandum from the Assistant Secretary of State for
Inter-American Affairs (Rubottom) to the Secretary of
State.
Washington, February 17, 1958.
SUBJECT
Consultation with Venezuela on Petroleum Imports Problem
Problem:
To obtain Venezuelan cooperation, or at least minimize Venezuelan
opposition to whatever steps are required by the U.S. Government to
solve the petroleum imports problem in view of mounting industry
and congressional pressure for restrictions which perhaps will have to
go beyond the present voluntary program.
Confidential. The source text is an initialed carbon copy.

Discussion:
While the voluntary restrictions of petroleum imports begun several
months ago has worked reasonably well, there are increasing signs that
the Congress may be inclined to give favorable consideration to more
severe restrictions in the form of tariffs and/or quotas which would seri-
ously damage our relations with Venezuela, Canada, and other oil produc-
ing countries, possibly as the price for extending the Trade Agreements
Act. The Venezuelan problem has been intensified by the coming into
production of new wells in concessions held by companies which have
just entered the Venezuelan picture. The pressure on the market of this
additional production at a time when demand for oil is slackening may
break the price structure at any time, according to informed observers.
Of the four alternatives ahead: (1) continued voluntary restric-
tions; (2) mandatory curbs imposed by Executive Act under Article 7 of
the Reciprocal Trade Agreements Act; (3) import restrictions imposed
by the Congress; and (4) export controls by Venezuela, No. 1 would
obviously be the best from our viewpoint as well as that of Venezuela.
developing situation. Although No. 3 would be ruled out as distasteful
to the U.S. oil industry, the application of Venezuelan good offices to
facilitate the partition of the U.S. import market from Venezuela among
the major and new producers might save the voluntary program.
Venezuela can be expected to be especially interested in the price factor.
It is very much in the U.S. interest to obtain Venezuelan under-
standing and support, if possible, of whatever program we embark
upon. The Perez Jimenez Government was restive under the voluntary
plan and the Embassy has reported that the present Junta Government
may react strongly to making the import controls mandatory. However,
every effort should be made to explain to the Venezuelans the nature
of the U.S. problem and to make this consultation meaningful rather
than simply informative. The Venezuelan Chargé Dr. Carlos Pérez de la
Cova has responded favorably to frank talks by Mr. Mann and myself.
We all agreed that it is essential that someone from this Government go
to Caracas to discuss the problem frankly with the Junta, the Cabinet,
and key political leaders. Subject to your approval, I will undertake
such a trip since I have not yet visited Caracas in my tenure as Assistant
Secretary. My having been the head of the U.S. negotiating team for the
revised trade agreement with Venezuela in 19522 could be expected to
facilitate the task considerably. This agreement, under which we are
now exporting nearly $1 billion annually to Venezuela, would probably
not survive unless Venezuelan cooperation is obtained.
American Republics.

Venezuela  1199
Recommendation:3
That you approve my holding as soon as possible discussions with
the Venezuelan Government and key political leaders in Caracas to
obtain their views with respect to the problem caused by the over pro-
duction of oil.
Approved by Secy.” Another notation on the source text indicates that Thomas C. Mann,
Assistant Secretary of State for Economic Affairs, Loftus E. Becker, Legal Adviser of the
Department, and Maurice M. Bernbaum, Director of the Office of South American Affairs,
were notified on February 19, that the memorandum had been approved.
reporting on his visit at the Secretary’s staff meeting, March 4, Rubottom stated in part
the following:
“In Venezuela the principal subjects which Mr. Rubottom discussed were the oil
imports question and the future of the oil industry. He said the new Venezuelan Minister
of Fuel is friendly to the U.S. and wishes to work out some arrangements, possibly
within a Western hemisphere mutual effort, to prevent a disastrous drop in fuel prices.
Secretary’s Staff Meetings, Lot 63 D 75).
VE–6.
Telegram from the Chargé in Venezuela (Burrows) to the
Department of State
636. Foreign Office delivered today note2 concerning petroleum restric-
tions, both present voluntary and possible future “obligatory adminis-
trative or legislative measures”. Summary 6 points raised:

(1) Although convinced present or future situation created by
restrictions can affect normal development trade under 1952 agree-
ment, Venezuela considers time inopportune raise this aspect problem.
(2) Venezuela considers indispensable any steps adopted be within
spirit complete understanding, harmony.
(3) Government believes measures taken will affect internal econ-
omy and fundamental aspects Venezuela life, petroleum being essential
factor. Without doubt measures will affect bilateral trade.
(4) From broader viewpoint observe that tendency towards restric-
tionist policy can unfavorably influence traditional principles coopera-
tion underlying hemispheric relations.
(5) Transcendence problem has caused impact on Venezuela public
opinion which showing concern over consequences that (restrictions)
have on “La Vida Nacional” notwithstanding confidence mutually sat-
isfactory solutions can be found.
(6) Government considers existing ties two countries require main-
tenance close permanent contact order achieve understanding between
parties respecting common problems and measures adopted solve
same. Therefore government views with special pleasure “idea sug-
gested by Rubottom that consultation between Venezuela and U.S. be
held” to study any measures affecting imports Venezuelan oil in U.S.

Editorial Note
On March 24, 1958, in a Supplementary Report to the Report of the
President’s Special Committee to Investigate Crude Oil Imports, dated July
29, 1957, the Special Committee recommended additional restrictions
on crude oil imports quotas for Districts I–IV, the area east of the
Rocky Mountains. The President approved the Special Committee’s
report in a memorandum dated March 25, 1958, and in similar memo-
approved the Special Committee’s recommendations relating to
1) importation of petroleum products other than crude, 2) allocations
for District V, the Pacific West Coast, and 3) continuance of existing
crude oil allocations through February 28, 1959. See Public Papers of the
Presidents of the United States: Dwight D. Eisenhower, 1958 (Washington:
1958), p. 230. Documentation concerning U.S. oil policy is printed in
Foreign Relations, 1958–1960, vol. IV, Foreign Economic Policy. Further
documentation is located in Department of State decimal files 411.006,
Policy” and “Oil U.S. Legislation”, and ARA/EST Files, Lot 62 D 8,
“Petroleum Policy” and “Oil Import Program.”

Caracas, April 18, 1958.
Dear Maurie:
With reference to your Telegram 6282 and our 742, I don’t believe
I can emphasize too much the strength of the feeling here on this par-
ticular issue.
Jimenez—Estrada.” Confidential; Official-Informal.
Patriotica, Vincente Lecuna, told Department officials that he feared demonstra-
tions during the Nixon visit if Pérez Jimenez and Pedro Estrada were still in the U.S.
tions with Venezuela caused by the presence in the United States of Pérez and Estrada and
stated, “Far from quieting down strength of feeling this issue being maintained and prob-

Unless something should happen prior to the Vice President’s
arrival in Caracas (and by “something” I mean the departure of Pérez
Jiménez and Pedro Estrada from the United States, temporarily if noth-
ing else), I am sure there will be demonstrations and perhaps other
unpleasant events. I don’t know quite how the Department would go
about this, but if there is some way to encourage these two to be out
of the United States while the Vice President is on this trip, I think it
might be one of the smartest things the Department could possibly do.
We read in the paper here yesterday that PJ was preparing to travel
to Switzerland and, as a matter of fact, that Mrs. Pérez Jiménez had
already undertaken negotiations to buy a house there. The Foreign
Minister in commenting on this yesterday said what a wonderful thing
it would be if the report were true. He, incidentally, expressed the hope
that there would not be any demonstrations while the Vice President
